note:
SpaceX在2021年首次执行任务期间发射了第17批Starlink卫星，使用的是猎鹰9号火箭，这是第八次飞行，并再次降落，记录了其可重复使用性计划的记录。这使Starlink星座的总规模几乎达到1,000，因为该公司已将服务的beta访问计划扩展到了英国和加拿大，并在后者公司中进行了首次部署，为该省偏远地区的农村原住民社区提供服务安大略省。
 发射是在美国东部标准时间上午8:02（太平洋标准时间上午5:02）从佛罗里达起飞的，卫星的发射按照计划在发射后约一个小时进行。如前所述，这次发射的助推器此前曾飞过7次，包括在12月将SiriusXM卫星送入轨道以支持该公司的卫星无线电网络时。
 今天的发射也是值得注意的，因为它包括在所谓的“信封膨胀”条件下进行着陆尝试，这意味着SpaceX的无人机回收船停泊在海上的着陆区的风实际上超出了公司先前定义的安全窗口。着陆尝试。
  由于今天的成功，SpaceX现在可能具有更高的风速容限，以便尝试恢复，这将意味着基于着陆区的天气情况取消发射的次数将减少。
note_en:
SpaceX has launched its 17th batch of Starlink satellites during its first mission of 2021, using a Falcon 9 rocket that was flying for the eighth time, and that landed again, recording a record for its reusability program. This puts the total Starlink constellation size at almost 1,000, as the company has expanded its beta access program for the service to the UK and Canada, with a first deployment in the latter company serving a rural First Nations community in a remote part of the province of Ontario.
 The launch took off from Florida at 8:02 AM EST (5:02 AM PST), with delivery of the satellites following as planned at around an hour after lift-off. The booster on this launch flew seven times previously, as mentioned – including just in December when it was used to delivery a SiriusXM satellite to orbit to support that company’s satellite radio network.
 Today’s launch was also notable because it included a landing attempt in so-called “envelope expansion” conditions, which means that the winds in the landing zone where SpaceX’s drone recovery ship was stationed at sea actually exceeded the company’s previously-defined safety window for making a landing attempt.
  As a result of today’s success, SpaceX will likely now have higher tolerances for wind speeds in order to attempt recovery, which should translate to fewer cancellations of launches based on weather conditions in the landing zone.
title: SpaceX将于2021年首次发射时再交付60颗Starlink卫星，并创造了新的Falcon 9火箭可重复使用性记录
title_en: SpaceX delivers 60 more Starlink satellites in first launch of 2021, and sets new Falcon 9 rocket reusability record

note:
Fyllo说，它已经与25个州（以及波多黎各和牙买加）的320家大麻零售商合作。根据首席营销官康拉德·利斯科（Conrad Lisco）的说法，此次收购使该公司能够提供该行业的“首个端到端营销解决方案”，将消费者数据，数字广告，法规遵从性结合在一起（这要归功于Fyllo去年对CannaRegs的收购），并通过将DataOwl，CRM和忠诚度绑定到企业的销售点系统中。
 例如，创始人兼首席执行官Chad Bronstein（以前是数字营销公司Amobee的首席营收官）说，零售商将能够使用Fyllo平台向普通客户发送促销文字，同时，至关重要的是，确保这些活动能够全面进行。遵守州和地方法规。他补充说，最终，该平台可以在大麻以外的其他受管制行业中使用。
  布朗斯坦说：“美容，赌博等-每个受管制的行业都需要做同样的事情，他们都会从忠诚度和合规性自动化中受益。”
 此外，他认为，主流品牌对使用大麻和CBD消费者的数据越来越感兴趣，正如Fyllo委托Forrester进行的一项研究所证实的那样。
  利斯科表示，此次收购是大麻行业的关键时刻，在许多州，药房被列为必不可少的业务，而且大麻合法化的势头仍在继续。
 他说：“到2020年，大麻已经成熟。” “我们会说它在10个月内从非法变成了必不可少的……2021年实际上是在观察流行的（大麻）品牌试图扩大规模，以便他们可以利用爆炸性增长来获利。从历史上看，它们已被其他非流行[主流]品牌在投放市场时会使用的整合营销功能所排斥。”
 Bronstein表示，Fyllo的目标是将这些功能引入大麻品牌，首先是将其合规功能引入DataOwl产品中。该公司还旨在建立一个全国性的大麻忠诚度平台，使一个州的大麻零售商可以轻松地以合规的方式将其营销能力扩展到其他州。 
收购的财务条款未披露。 DataOwl的联合创始人Dan Hirsch和Vartan Arabyan和其他团队一样都加入了Fyllo，使公司的总员工人数达到110名。  赫希在一份声明中说：“通过与Fyllo集成，DataOwl的解决方案将通过业界最富创新性的营销平台吸引尽可能多的受众。”
note_en:
Fyllo said it already works with 320 cannabis retailers across 25 states (plus Puerto Rico and Jamaica). According to Chief Marketing Officer Conrad Lisco, this acquisition allows the company to offer the industry’s “first end-to-end marketing solution,” combining consumer data, digital advertising, regulatory compliance (thanks to  Fyllo’s acquisition of CannaRegs last year) and, through DataOwl, CRM and loyalty tied into a business’ point-of-sale system.
 As an example, founder and CEO Chad Bronstein (previously the chief revenue officer at digital marketing company Amobee) said that retailers will be able to use the Fyllo platform to send promotional texts to regular customers while, crucially, ensuring that those campaigns are fully in compliance with state and local regulations. He added that eventually, the platform could be used beyond cannabis, in other regulated industries.
  “Beauty, gambling, etc. — the same things need to happen in every regulated industry, they would all benefit from loyalty and compliance automation,” Bronstein said.
 In addition, he argued that mainstream brands are increasingly interested in using data around cannabis and CBD consumers, as borne out in  a Forrester study commissioned by Fyllo.
  Lisco said this acquisition comes at a crucial time for the cannabis industry, with dispensaries classified as  essential businesses in many states, as well as  continuing momentum behind marijuana legalization.
 “In 2020, cannabis came of age,” he said. “We would say it went form illicit to essential in 10 months … 2021 is really about watching endemic [marijuana] brands try to scale, so that they can capitalize on the explosive growth. They’ve historically been excluded from the kinds of integrated marketing capabilities that other non-endemic [mainstream] brands get to use when go to market.”
 Bronstein said Fyllo aims to bring those capabilities to marijuana brands, first by bringing the its compliance capabilities into the DataOwl product. The company also aims to create a national cannabis loyalty platform, allowing a marijuana retailer in one state to easily expand its marketing capabilities into other states in a compliant fashion.
 The financial terms of the acquisition were not disclosed. DataOwl co-founders Dan Hirsch and Vartan Arabyan are joining Fyllo, as is the rest of their team, bringing the company’s total headcount to 110.
 “By integrating with Fyllo, DataOwl’s solutions will reach the widest possible audience via the industry’s most innovative marketing platform,” Hirsch said in a statement.
title: 大麻营销启动表Askures TataOol
title_en: Cannabis marketing startup Fyllo acquires DataOwl

note:
在赢得11月大选的几天后，乔·拜登（Joe Biden）宣布了担任其过渡时期人员的姓名。大技术公司占据了突出的位置。其中服务于总统当选该机构审查小组的数百人员，有一个从尤伯杯，两名来自亚马逊，一个来自谷歌。然后有两个人来自叛逆防御，这是一家影子防御初创公司。
  这一消息使华盛顿内部人士争先恐后地寻找该公司。名单上没有主要的国防承包商。科技公司甲骨文执行副总裁肯·格鲁克（Ken Glueck）告诉我：“像Rebellion这样成立了一年的初创公司最终有两名员工在总统过渡团队中工作，这真是奇怪。”
  什么是叛乱防御？以《星球大战》的典故为名，这家公司不是您的典型承包商。 Rebellion于2019年夏季推出，旨在为国防工业制造人工智能（AI）软件。贸易出版物赞扬它的创新性。在董事会成员埃里克·施密特（Eric Sc​​hmidt）的大力支持下，它迅速筹集了6300万美元。施密特以Google的前首席执行官而闻名，但他还是亿万富翁投资者，还是重要政府机构的有影响力的顾问。
    施密特（Schmidt）担任白宫和国会咨询委员会主席，该委员会被称为国家安全人工智能委员会。在官方职位上，他主张国防部和情报机构采用更多的机器学习技术。同时，作为风险投资家，他已经向数十家国家安全初创公司投资了数百万美元，这些初创公司将这些技术卖给了政府。
  政府监督机构认为这些双重角色存在利益冲突。非营利组织“电子隐私信息中心”高级顾问约翰·戴维森说：“他有很多很多经济激励措施，以确保国防部和其他联邦机构积极采用AI。”
  拜登政府需要谨慎行事，以免大技术公司接管政府职能。在奥巴马任职初期，谷歌代表每周参加一次以上的白宫会议，使一些人开玩笑地称政府为Google.gov。在奥巴马任职期间，超过250名Google员工在公司和政府之间来回迁移。施密特现在准备在新的白宫中发挥更大的作用。
  GOOGLE在90年代迅速发展起来。董事会于2001年聘请埃里克·施密特（Eric Sc​​hmidt）担任首席执行官，为这家初创公司的怪异创始人提供了商人优势。早期技术的最高执行官施密特（Schmidt）曾担任Sun Microsystems和Novell十年。随后，他成为Google的新母公司Alphabet的执行董事长，这家极具价值的公开交易搜索引擎巨头将业务扩展到了人工智能和国家安全等新领域。 
施密特体现了科技与政府之间的纽带。他是奥巴马白宫的常客，并向总统候选人希拉里·克林顿（Hillary Clinton）发送了战略备忘录。当奥巴马的Healthcare.gov网站过热时，政府将他带进来以清理混乱。 2016年11月，施密特在克林顿的选举之夜聚会上戴了“工作人员”徽章。
  您的捐款使该网站免费开放，以供所有人阅读。尽你所能...
  对于施密特来说，大奖是国家安全部门。在1960年代和70年代，国防部是迄今为止全国最大的研发投资国。互联网当然首先是一个军事平台。但是到了1990年代，国防部在大型武器系统上的投入超过了在创新下一件大事上的花费。中央情报局和国家安全局通过研究渠道，在90年代初向Google提供了种子资金，但是不久之后，最新的创新技术便从硅谷传到了政府，而不是反过来。到2003年，在Google技术支持随附的一个项目中，Google将技术卖给了NSA，以帮助其对大量数据进行分类。
  五角大楼和情报机构已经落后。这部分与国防官僚机构的僵化，从科技公司引进外部人才的困难以及政府与大型国防公司签订合同的复杂过程有关。一直以来，私营部门在新研究上的支出几乎是国防部的5：1。
  奥巴马宣誓就职八天后，他邀请施密特（Schmidt）提出局外人关于如何像科技公司一样开展国防业务的观点。奥巴马最后的国防部长阿什·卡特（Ash Carter）为施密特（Schmidt）在国防创新委员会上设立了一个职位，该职位正在推动私有部门中的主流事务，但在政府机构中被视为激进分子，国防部需要新软件。卡特和施密特共同选择了其他顾问。
  当时，谷歌母公司的执行董事长施密特突然获得了政治领导和全球军事行动的空前机会。他前往全球约100个军事设施，在那里他迅速指出了五角大楼的技术局限性。到奥巴马第二任期结束时，施密特已经在国家安全圈内聚集了巨大的影响力。五角大楼新成立的联合人工智能中心的创始人杰克·沙纳汉中将告诉我，施密特是“我们的良师益友”。
  作为董事会主席，施密特推动奥巴马政府将人工智能，新软件和云计算引入该部门。他“用手指指着国防部长的胸口，‘你不明白。您看不到外面正在发生什么。人工智能将改变我们所做的一切，而你们将陷入过去。’” Shanahan说。 
2017年，谷歌从五角大楼赢得了一份价值1700万美元的合同，使用人工智能来检查无人机镜头，以使无人机能够更精确地筛查潜在目标。一年后，该公司的十几名员工辞职以示抗议。施密特（Schmidt）提倡大技术公司（Big Tech）在国家安全中发挥更大的作用，现在他自己的工程师使他感到尴尬。 （他说，他在让Google获得政府合同方面没有任何作用。）Shanahan中将执行了该计划，该计划被称为Project Maven。在新闻报道中，“有争议”一词几乎总是贴在上面。
  您的捐款使该网站免费开放，以供所有人阅读。尽你所能...
 施密特已经彻底分裂了两党。在特朗普第一次召集技术主管时，他坐在史蒂夫·班农（Steve Bannon）旁边，并很快提倡特朗普政府在人工智能方面做更多工作。 2018年，国会在其年度军事资助法案中建立了一个独立的人工智能委员会，该委员会将在五角大楼之外运作并为国会提供建议。董事会的第一任主席是埃里克·施密特（Eric Sc​​hmidt）。
  施密特（Schmidt）加入新成立的国家人工智能安全委员会后，便开始要求国会在一切事物中使用AI。一位了解委员会日常运作情况的人士说：“这真是一场秀。”专员热衷于推广AI，因此对于适用于不同机构和部门的复杂法律知之甚少。这位人士称该委员会“经常用沙鼠轮来产生无用的内容。”
  该委员会可能会引起利益冲突，它的报告托管在Google云端硬盘上，并使用Gmail作为其电子邮件平台。一位发言人告诉我，该委员会的工作人员选择了Google联邦批准的办公产品套件，而施密特对此没有任何投入。
  副主席鲍勃·沃克（Bob Work）在一份声明中说：“委员会成员共同在我们的会议上监视潜在的利益冲突，并确保我们的对话不会转向对特定商业利益的不正当讨论。”
  “施密特在这个领域中拥有巨大的力量，我们认为这非常令人震惊。这似乎是明显的利益冲突，”隐私保护非营利组织EPIC的律师戴维森说。 “这使一个奇怪的人感到奇怪，他担任这个委员会的负责人很多。” 
该委员会的每位成员均提交道德操守披露。对于大多数专员而言，这些文档的篇幅为7至11页；施密特的财务披露长达38页。
  甚至拒绝通过发言人与《展望》进行对话的施密特本人也承认可能会造成混乱。 2019年11月，他主持委员会第一次公开活动时提到了潜在的冲突。施密特开玩笑说：“这真是一场悲剧，我们再也没有戴帽子了。”施密特对着Google的一位高级官员和坐在他旁边的尚纳汉中将打手势。 “我可能是全世界唯一可以说这句话的人：我与他们一起工作，并为他们俩工作。”当然，当他试图在这两个角色之间切换时，很明显他根本没有戴帽子。不可能说出他是在说谁。
  施密特说：“科技界普遍担心，军工联合企业会以某种方式误用其物品杀死他人，”
  当他们三个人谈论Google为国防部所做的工作时，施密特有效地利用了政府活动向国防和AI专家的受众推销Google的服务。他要求将军和谷歌官员分别解释导致谷歌工程师辞职的无人机计划Project Maven如何比媒体报道的还要成功。
  施密特于2019年6月离开了Alphabet。他越来越不认同工程师对国防工作的看法，他本人也承认这种不匹配。施密特说：“科技界普遍担心，军工联合企业会以某种方式误用其物品杀死他人，”
  他提出了一个解决方案：科技企业家应该建立新的公司来履行士兵和间谍的合同。他说：“我的猜测是，将会成立更多与军事使命和价值观保持一致的科技公司。”
  他将其付诸实践。退休一个月后，施密特（Schmidt）的风险投资公司投资了Rebellion Defense。 
REBELLION的创始人以五角大楼的专门部门为公司模范，该部门有许多人在离开该公司开始创办新企业之前就曾工作过。该部门是一个名为“国防数字服务”（Defense Digital Service）的小型行动，该行动从公司招募了软件专家来进行短期服务。施密特是早期的拥护者，他于2018年4月告诉国会，该部门应扩大到数百人。
  国防数字服务局的成员将自己视为五角大楼内部的一群局外人。他们甚至在单位门上贴了一块标有“反叛联盟”的牌匾。它的第一任董事是来自西雅图的技术人员克里斯·林奇（Chris Lynch），他创立了许多令人难忘的创业公司，包括文字游戏应用程序和一个名为CelebHookup的热门网站。林奇（Lynch）在奥巴马倡议的美国数字服务部（US Digital Service）中失去了竞争优势，然后迅速将其晋升为五角大楼的新职位。林奇说话很快，并部署了嗡嗡声的口号。他竭尽全力抨击不想在杀人机器上工作的Google工程师。
  ×克里斯·林奇（Chris Lynch）在2016年担任五角大楼国防数字服务部总监
 林奇在特朗普执政期间继续为国防数字服务部工作，并负责领导五角大楼过渡到单一云，这是一个极其复杂的100亿美元的JEDI计划。 （JEDI合同最终归微软所有，因为据称特朗普亲自否决了亚马逊作为主要供应商。）
  既然他已经学会了如何驾驭国防部，林奇便着手进入私营部门。他于2019年与仍在五角大楼工作的英国对手奥利弗·刘易斯（Oliver Lewis）和妮可·卡马里洛（Nicole Camarillo）共同创立了叛乱防御组织。为投资者准备的宣传台吹捧“她目前在美国陆军网络司令部的领导角色”。这三人开始呼吁风险资本家提供资金。
    叛乱称自己为“现代曼哈顿计划”。他们强调了他们对五角大楼和国会的最新了解，认为这是“早期初创企业试图制造将出售给政府的产品的重大开端”。他们的三个初始实验室将包括将AI用于军事和警务，保护大数据系统以及创建最终的防病毒系统。每个人将价值数十亿美元。 Rebellion的创始人写道：“国防的使命一定是在高科技领域。” “这是不受限制的'Project Maven'。” Rebellion会做Google不会做的。球场似乎是为埃里克·施密特（Eric Sc​​hmidt）设计的。
  一位不愿透露姓名的风险投资家说，他不想破坏现有的关系。 Rebellion没有提供新产品。它的创始人出售了他们在五角大楼的专业知识，在内部合同方面的工作经验以及与高级黄铜的关系。这位投资者告诉我：“在其他初创公司中，这显然是为了吸引优秀的军事和情报AI承包商而设计的。” “如果您能用侧面带有字母“ AI”的大锤子，他们会给您一袋钱。” 
Rebellion将自己定位为草率的Luke Skywalker的奉献者，但其支持者看起来更像Darth Vader。在2019年，该公司在谋杀者排行的风险投资家的帮助下筹集了6300万美元，比创始人最初希望获得的1300万美元多。由特朗普盟友彼得·泰尔（Peter Thiel）共同创立的创始人基金会（Founders Fund）参与进来。小报大亨的儿子詹姆斯·默多克（James Murdoch）也是如此，该公司的投资公司Lupa Systems一直在收购媒体。泰德·史莱因（Ted Schlein）在Rebellion董事会中鲜为人知，可能更具影响力。 Schlein是CIA支持的风险投资公司In-Q-Tel的受托人，他深入了解了间谍机构所投资的内容。然后，其中最重的打击者是Eric Sc​​hmidt。
  Rebellion建立了一个网站，其中列出了所有关键人员。对于董事会成员埃里克·施密特（Eric Sc​​hmidt），没有简历。相反，在他的照片旁边有一个链接，直接链接到他在国防创新委员会的简介，该机构向国会和五角大楼就如何为Rebellion出售的确切技术分配资源提供建议。它建议在施密特为政府和私营部门开展的工作之间没有防火墙。很快，施密特将在叛军办公室旁聊天。
  Rebellion的创始人正在出售他们在五角大楼的专有技术，在内部合同上的工作经验以及与高级黄铜的关系。
  该公司的网站上标有针对国家安全客户的口号（“叛军为战士而建”）以及将引起硅谷工程师共鸣的口号（“加入叛军”）。林奇甚至将他在华盛顿特区公寓电话亭上的问候语改为“我们是叛乱”。 （叛乱防御组织通过发言人拒绝对此记录发表评论或拒绝让工作人员面试。）
  除了主要投资人和团队中穿着连帽衫的黑客类型外，Rebellion还需要国防部扣留的前高管。它的业务部门由“国防官僚黑客的常设团队”和“政府采购专家”组成，这些人完全有资格获得令人垂涎的国防合同。鲍勃·戴格（Bob Daigle）是五角大楼的有钱人。从2017年到2019年，他担任成本评估和计划评估办公室主任，这是他在乔治·W·布什政府任职期间担任的高级职务。 Daigle的工作是确保每个项目都在预算之内，现在他是Rebellion的创始主管。托尼·埃拉迪（Tony Ierardi）曾为参谋长联席会议做过类似的工作。在服役近40年后，他于2019年秋季退休，成为中将参谋长。 （就像Schmidt，Daigle和Ierardi一样，还有时间从事其他工作。他们俩都是Pallas Advisors的顾问，Jim Mattis的技术顾问Sally Donnelly于去年成立，其前任高级管理人员阵容强大。）Rebellion的另一位顾问David Recordon根据他的简介，他在奥巴马白宫负责IT事务，并为行政人员制定了“现代化的合同战略”。前国防部长阿什·卡特（Ash Carter）也为公司提供建议。
  创始人曾告诉投资者，他们将“为全球国防工业创造数十亿美元的挑战者”。到2020年，Rebellion的合同越来越多。它在西雅图增加了一个办公室，并扩大了在伦敦的业务，最近又在伦敦招聘了国防部前参谋长。 “我们正在招聘（很多），”一位员工在11月在LinkedIn上发布。 Rebellion在社交媒体平台上列出了85名员工，并提供19个职位空缺。对于初创公司来说，这是一个很大的飞跃。
  叛乱的行为就像一家科技公司。它强调淡淡的文化，播放《星球大战》的参考资料，并拍摄有意俗气的圣诞节视频，其中每个员工都与Mariah Carey一起唱歌。实际上，这是一家国防公司。 
政府将奖金拨给Rebellion，部分原因是该公司赞助了著名的研究，称政府应使用其产品。例如，在2020年，叛乱基金会（Rebellion）为战略与国际研究中心提供了资金，以敦促国防部门在收集和分析情报方面使用更多的机器学习方法，这正是叛乱所兜售的确切产品。 （CSIS发言人说：“我们支持学者的独立性和分析质量。”）如今，叛乱一直在为撰写这些智囊团报告而付费的人们也加入了拜登政府。由叛军资助的CSIS工作组的联合主席是现任拜登国家情报总监的艾薇儿·海恩斯（Avril Haines）；它的网站上将凯瑟琳·希克斯（Kathleen Hicks）列为高级顾问，拜登（Biden）在12月宣布，她将成为五角大楼的第二名。
  拜登政府现已确保SCHMIDT的影响力。去年9月，他曾在拜登（Biden）竞选筹款活动中，与前国防部官员米歇尔·弗洛诺（MichèleFlournoy）一起演讲，他一直在为施密特的慈善事业提供咨询。现在，施密特正在为那些在国家安全岗位上任职的人提供服务。通过他的慈善组织施密特期货（Schmidt Futures）并在咨询公司WestExec Advisors的前奥巴马官员的建议下，他发起了一项倡议，旨在向五角大楼和情报机构注入更多的技术人才。
  Rebellion的未来有两种保障方式：通过合同和联系。去年11月，它赢得了可能成为该公司迄今为止最大奖项的合同，该合同是为空军创建一个单一的数据共享网络。然后有过渡团队宣布，该公司加入了亚马逊和谷歌的大联盟。
  一年前，Rebellion在最初向投资者宣传时说：“国防部及其盟国内部的牵引是成功的主要动力-任务至关重要。”现在，按照他们自己的条件，他们赢了。
  Yelp在华盛顿负责公共政策工作的路德·洛（Luther Lowe）表示：“至少有两个人加入了登陆部队，这真是令人大跌眼镜。” （Yelp一直是Google的坚定批评者，

note_en:
Days after winning the November election, Joe Biden announced the names of those staffing his transition. Big Tech landed prominent spots. Among the hundreds of personnel on the agency review teams serving the president-elect, there was one from Uber, two from Amazon, and one from Google. And then there were two people from Rebellion Defense, a shadowy defense startup.
  The announcement sent Washington insiders scrambling to look up the company. No major defense contractors appeared on the list. “It’s sure odd that a year-old startup like Rebellion winds up with two employees serving on a presidential transition team,” Ken Glueck, the executive vice president of the tech company Oracle, told me.
  What is Rebellion Defense? With a  Star Wars allusion as its name, this firm is not your typical contractor. Rebellion launched in the summer of 2019 to craft artificial-intelligence (AI) software for the defense industry. Trade publications gushed about how innovative it was. It quickly raised $63 million, with the conspicuous backing of its board member Eric Schmidt. Schmidt is best known as the former CEO of Google, but he’s also a billionaire investor and an influential consultant to key government bodies.
    Schmidt serves as chairman of an advisory board to the White House and Congress called the National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence. From official positions, he has advocated for the Defense Department and intelligence agencies to adopt more machine-learning technology. Meanwhile, as a venture capitalist, he has invested millions of dollars in more than a half-dozen national-security startups that sell those very technologies back to the government.
  Government watchdogs consider those dual roles a conflict of interest. “He’s got many, many financial incentives to ensure that the Department of Defense and other federal agencies adopt AI aggressively,” said John Davisson, senior counsel for the nonprofit Electronic Privacy Information Center.
  The Biden administration will need to tread carefully to avoid Big Tech taking over functions of government. Early in Obama’s presidency, Google representatives  attended more than one White House meeting a week, leading some to jokingly call the administration Google.gov. More than 250 Google employees moved back and forth between the company and government during the Obama years. Schmidt is now poised to have even more sway within the new White House.
  GOOGLE RAPIDLY GREW in the ’90s. Its board hired Eric Schmidt as CEO in 2001 to offer a businessman’s edge to the startup’s eccentric founders. Schmidt, a top executive at early tech stalwarts Sun Microsystems and Novell, served in that role for a decade. He then became executive chairman of Google’s new parent company, Alphabet, as the highly valuable, publicly traded search engine giant expanded into new fields, like artificial intelligence and national security.
  Schmidt personifies the bonds between tech and government. He was a frequent visitor to the Obama White House and sent strategy memos to presidential candidate Hillary Clinton. When Obama’s healthcare.gov website overheated, the administration brought him in to clean up the mess. In November 2016, Schmidt wore a “staff” badge at Clinton’s election-night party.
  Your donation keeps this site free and open for all to read. Give what you can...
  For Schmidt, the big prize was the national-security sector. In the 1960s and ’70s, the Defense Department was by far the largest investor in research and development nationwide. The internet, of course, was at first a military platform. But by the 1990s, the Defense Department was spending more money on big weapons systems than on innovating the next big thing.  The CIA and NSA had, through research conduits, provided seed money to Google in the early ’90s, but soon the latest innovations were coming from Silicon Valley to government, not the other way around. By 2003, Google was selling technology to the NSA to help it sort through a barrage of data, in a project that came with Google tech support.
  The Pentagon and intelligence agencies had fallen behind. Part of it had to do with the rigidity of the defense bureaucracy, the difficulty of bringing in outside talent from tech companies, and the convoluted processes of government contracting that privilege massive defense companies. All the while, the private sector was outspending the Defense Department almost 5 to 1 on new research.
  Eight days after Obama was sworn in, he brought on Schmidt to offer an outsider’s view on how to run defense operations more like a tech company. Obama’s final defense secretary, Ash Carter, created a position for Schmidt on the Defense Innovation Board, which was pushing for what was mainstream in the private sector, but seen as radical in government offices—that the Defense Department needed new software. Carter and Schmidt  jointly selected the other advisers.
  Schmidt, then the executive chairman of Google’s parent company, had suddenly obtained unprecedented access to political leadership and global military operations. He traveled to some  100 military installations worldwide, where he was quick to point out the Pentagon’s technological limitations. By the end of Obama’s second term, Schmidt had gathered a huge amount of influence within national-security circles. Lt. Gen. Jack Shanahan, the founding head of the Pentagon’s newly formed Joint Artificial Intelligence Center, told me that Schmidt was “a mentor to us.”
  As board chair, Schmidt pushed the Obama administration to bring artificial intelligence, new software, and cloud computing into the department. He was “poking a finger in the secretary of defense’s chest saying, ‘You don’t get it. You don’t see what’s happening on the outside. AI is going to transform everything we do and you guys are stuck in the past,’” Shanahan said.
  In 2017, Google won a $17 million contract from the Pentagon to examine drone footage using artificial intelligence, to enable drones to sift through potential targets with more precision. A year later, a dozen of the company’s employees resigned in protest. Schmidt had promoted Big Tech playing a bigger role in national security, and now his own engineers had embarrassed him. (He said he had played no role in getting Google the government contract.) Lt. Gen. Shanahan ran that initiative, which became known as Project Maven. In press reports, the word “controversial” was almost always tagged onto it.
  Your donation keeps this site free and open for all to read. Give what you can...
 Schmidt has been thoroughly bipartisan. He  sat next to Steve Bannon at Trump’s first convening of tech executives, and soon advocated that the Trump administration do more with AI. In 2018, Congress in its annual military-funding bill established an independent commission on artificial intelligence that would operate out of the Pentagon and advise Congress. The board’s first chairperson was Eric Schmidt.
  Once Schmidt joined the new National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence, he started telling Congress to use AI in everything. “It’s kind of a shitshow,” said someone with knowledge of the commission’s day-to-day operations. Eager to proselytize AI, commissioners knew little about the complicated laws that apply to different agencies and departments. The person described the commission as “constantly on a gerbil wheel generating content that was useless.”
  The commission, in what lends the appearance of conflict of interest, hosts its reports on Google Drive and uses Gmail as its email platform. A spokesperson informed me that the commission’s staff selected Google’s federally approved suite of office products “without any input” from Schmidt.
  Vice Chair Bob Work said in a statement, “Collectively, the Commission members monitor for potential conflicts of interest at our meetings and ensure our conversations do not veer into improper discussions of particular commercial interests.”
  “Schmidt wields a tremendous amount of power in this space, and we thought it was pretty alarming. It seemed like an obvious conflict of interest,” said Davisson, the lawyer with the privacy advocacy nonprofit EPIC. “It spoke to the strangeness of someone with so many financial entanglements chairing this commission.”
  Each member of the commission submits an ethics disclosure. For most commissioners, these documents run between 7 and 11 pages; Schmidt’s financial disclosure is 38 pages long.
  Even Schmidt himself, who declined to speak with the  Prospect through spokespeople, has acknowledged that there can be confusion. In November 2019, he alluded to his potential conflicts when he moderated the commission’s first public event. “It’s a real tragedy we don’t wear hats anymore,” Schmidt  joked as he gestured toward a top Google official and Lt. Gen. Shanahan sitting next to him. “I’m probably the only person who can say this in the entire world: I work with and for both of them.” Of course, as he tried to toggle between those two roles, it was clear that he was wearing no hat at all. It was impossible to tell on whose behalf he was speaking.
  “There’s a general concern in the tech community of somehow the military-industrial complex using their stuff to kill people incorrectly, if you will,” Schmidt said.
  As the three of them talked through the work Google had done for the Defense Department, Schmidt effectively used the government event to market Google’s services to the audience of defense and AI experts. He asked the general and the Google official to each explain how Project Maven, the drone program that had led to the resignation of Google engineers, was even more successful than the media had reported.
  Schmidt left Alphabet in June 2019. He was increasingly out of step with his engineers’ views of defense work, a mismatch that he himself admitted. “There’s a general concern in the tech community of somehow the military-industrial complex using their stuff to kill people incorrectly, if you will,” Schmidt  said.
  He proposed a solution: Tech entrepreneurs should establish new companies to fulfill contracts for soldiers and spies. “My guess is what will happen is that there will be tech companies founded that are more in alignment with the mission and values of the military,” he said.
  And he put that into practice. A month after retiring, Schmidt’s venture capital firm invested in Rebellion Defense.
  REBELLION’S FOUNDERS MODELED the company after the specialized Pentagon unit where many of them had worked before leaving to start the new business enterprise. The unit was a small operation called the Defense Digital Service, which brought in software experts from companies for short tours of service. Schmidt was an early fan, and  he told Congress in April 2018 that the unit should be expanded to hundreds of people.
  Members of the Defense Digital Service saw themselves as a band of outsiders within the Pentagon. They even put a plaque that said “Rebel Alliance” on the unit’s door. Its first director was Chris Lynch, a techie from Seattle who had founded a number of forgettable startups, including a word-game app and a hot-or-not site called CelebHookup. Lynch failed upward into a competitive position in the U.S. Digital Service, an Obama initiative, and then quickly parlayed that into a newly fashioned role at the Pentagon. Lynch spoke quickly and deployed buzzy slogans. He went out of his way to lambaste Google engineers who didn’t want to work on killing machines.
  ×      Chris Lynch in 2016 when he was director of the Pentagon’s Defense Digital Service
 Lynch continued to work for the Defense Digital Service during the Trump administration and was given responsibility for leading the Pentagon’s transition to a single cloud, the incredibly complex $10 billion JEDI program. (The JEDI contract ended up going to Microsoft, after Trump allegedly personally vetoed Amazon as a prime vendor.)
  Now that he had learned how to navigate the Defense Department, Lynch set out to move into the private sector. He co-founded Rebellion Defense in 2019 with British counterpart Oliver Lewis and Nicole Camarillo, who was still working for the Pentagon. A pitch deck for investors touted “her present leadership role in U.S. Army Cyber Command.” The trio started calling around to venture capitalists for funding.
    Rebellion called themselves “a modern day Manhattan Project.” They emphasized their recent knowledge of the Pentagon and Congress as a “substantial head start to the early-stage startups attempting to build products that are to be sold into government.” Their three initial labs would include using AI for the military and policing, protecting large data systems, and creating the ultimate antivirus system. Each one would be worth billions of dollars. “The mission of national defense must be  the place to be in tech,” Rebellion’s founders wrote. “This is an unconstrained ‘Project Maven.’” Rebellion would do what Google would not. The pitch seemed tailor-made for Eric Schmidt.
  One venture capitalist, who declined to be identified because he said he did not want to jeopardize existing relationships, said the pitch was weak. Rebellion wasn’t providing a new product. Its founders were selling their know-how of the Pentagon, their experience working on contracts on the inside, and their relationships with senior brass. “It was pretty obvious that this among other startups were designed to capture outstanding military and intelligence AI contractors,” the investor told me. “If you can offer a big hammer with the letters ‘AI’ on the side, they’ll hand you a bag of money.”
  Rebellion positioned itself as devotees of scrappy Luke Skywalker, but its backers looked a lot more like Darth Vader. In 2019, the firm raised $63 million with help from a murderers’ row of venture capitalists, $13 million more than the founders had hoped to initially secure. The Founders Fund, co-founded by Trump ally Peter Thiel, chipped in. So did James Murdoch, son of the tabloid magnate, whose investment firm Lupa Systems has been buying up media outlets. Ted Schlein, a lesser-known name on Rebellion’s board, was perhaps even more influential. Schlein is a trustee of the CIA-backed venture capital firm In-Q-Tel, and brings in-depth knowledge of what spy agencies are investing in. And then there was the heaviest hitter of them all, Eric Schmidt.
  Rebellion set up a website where it listed all of its key personnel. For board member Eric Schmidt, there was no bio. Instead, beside his photo there was a link that led directly to his profile on the Defense Innovation Board, the body advising Congress and the Pentagon on how to allocate resources toward the exact technology Rebellion was selling. It suggested that there was no firewall between Schmidt’s work for the government and the private sector. Soon, Schmidt would be dropping by the Rebellion office to chat.
  Rebellion’s founders were selling their know-how of the Pentagon, their experience working on contracts on the inside, and their relationships with senior brass.
  The company’s website had slogans to target national-security customers (“Rebellion Defense builds for the warfighter”) as well as ones that would resonate with Silicon Valley engineers (“Join the Rebellion”). Lynch even changed the greeting on his D.C. apartment’s call box to “We are the Rebellion.” (Rebellion Defense through a spokesperson declined to comment on the record or make staff available for interviews.)
  In addition to the major investors and the hoodie-wearing hacker types on their team, Rebellion also needed buttoned-up former executives from the Defense Department. Its business side was composed of “a permanent team of national defense bureaucracy hackers” and “government procurement experts”—people who were perfectly placed to snag highly coveted defense contracts. Bob Daigle has been the money man at the Pentagon. From 2017 to 2019, he served as the director of the Cost Assessment and Program Evaluation office, an office he had worked in at a senior level during the George W. Bush administration. Daigle’s job was to make sure every project was in budget, and now he was a founding executive of Rebellion. Tony Ierardi had been doing similar work for the Joint Chiefs of Staff. He retired as a lieutenant general in fall 2019 after almost four decades in the military and became the company’s chief of staff. (Like Schmidt, Daigle and Ierardi had time for another job, too. Both serve as consultants for Pallas Advisors, which  Jim Mattis’s tech consigliere Sally Donnelly founded last year with a formidable roster of former top officials.) Another adviser to Rebellion, David Recordon, directed IT in the Obama White House and “modernized contracting strategies” for administration staff, according to his profile. Former Defense Secretary Ash Carter also advises the company.
  The founders had told investors that they would “create a multi-billion dollar challenger to the global defense industry,” and by 2020, Rebellion was getting contracts and growing. It added an office in Seattle and expanded its presence in London, where it recently recruited the former chief of staff of the Ministry of Defense. “We’re hiring (a lot),” one staffer posted on LinkedIn in November. Rebellion has 85 employees listed on the social media platform—and 19 job openings. This was quite a leap for a startup.
  Rebellion acts like a tech company. It emphasizes its jaunty culture, plays up the  Star Wars references, and filmed an intentionally cheesy Christmas video where staffers each sing along to Mariah Carey. In actual fact, this is a defense company.
  Government awards flow to Rebellion in part because the company sponsored high-profile research that says the government should use their products. For example, in 2020, Rebellion funded the Center for Strategic and International Studies to urge the defense sector to use more machine learning in collecting and analyzing intelligence—the exact products that Rebellion peddles. (A CSIS spokesperson said, “We stand behind the independence of our scholars and the quality of their analysis.”) Now, the people that Rebellion has been paying to write those think-tank reports are joining the Biden administration, too. The co-chair of that  Rebellion-funded task force at CSIS was Avril Haines, now Biden’s director of national intelligence; its website listed Kathleen Hicks as a senior adviser, and Biden announced in December that she would be number two at the Pentagon.
  SCHMIDT’S INFLUENCE is now assured within the Biden administration. In September, he was a featured speaker at a Biden campaign fundraiser with  Michèle Flournoy, a former defense official who has been consulting for Schmidt’s philanthropy. Now, Schmidt is feeding the pipeline of those in national-security positions who will owe him their start in the business. Through his charitable organization Schmidt Futures and with the advice of former Obama officials at the consulting firm  WestExec Advisors, he is launching an initiative to seed more tech talent into the Pentagon and intelligence agencies.
  Rebellion’s future is secured in two ways—through contracts and connections. In November, it won what could grow into the company’s biggest award to date, a contract to create a single data-sharing network for the Air Force. And then there was the transition team announcement, where the company joined the big leagues of Amazon and Google.
  A year before, in its initial pitch to investors, Rebellion had said, “Traction inside of the Department of Defense and its allies is the primary driver of success—mission matters.” Now, by their own terms, they’ve won.
  “The fact that they got two people on the landing teams was eyebrow-raising to say the least,” said Luther Lowe, who runs Yelp’s public-policy efforts in Washington. (Yelp has been a persistent critic of Google, and by

title: 看看默默无闻的国防初创公司Rebellion Defense，该公司致力于为美国政府创建AI和Infosec应用程序，并得到Eric Schmidt的支持
title_en: A look at obscure defense startup Rebellion Defense, which works to create AI and infosec applications for the US government and is backed by Eric Schmidt

   英格兰的一个保护项目正在寻求雇用两名护林员来监督一小群四到十只欧洲野牛，该野牛将在2022年春季被引入坎特伯雷附近的古代布莱恩森林。该地区尚未见到野牛漫游。野生估计有6,000年的历史。
  斯坦·史密斯（Stan Smith）是肯特野生动物基金会（Kent Wildlife Trust）的野生动物景观经理，该组织与怀尔德伍德基金会（Wildwood Trust）合作创建了Wilder Blean项目，以重新引入野牛。他希望这项倡议将增加那里的生态多样性，并正在寻找人类帮助实现这一目标。
  我们不希望人们在英国有野牛的经历，这是肯定的，因为您还没有得到。这里没有任何人，他告诉了来自英国黑斯廷斯的主持人Carol Off。
  但是，我们在人中寻找的是对动物非常善解人意的人，他们可能习惯于管理大型放牧食草动物，例如牛或马，以及其他人真正了解...该地区的生态的人，＆＃34;他说。
    史密斯说，以前，该地区原本是绝种的草原野牛的故乡。
  新的野牛群将有大约200公顷的土地要放牧，大约是林地400公顷的一半。根据史密斯的说法，它们将被电围栏保持住。 
这是一个非常大的林地，是我们在英格兰南部仍然拥有的最大的古代林地之一，＆＃34;史密斯说。
    希望这些动物将通过其自然放牧行为使林地生态系统受益。
  史密斯说，野牛是自然的＆＃34;生态系统工程师＆＃34;仅仅因为在那里而改变了他们的栖息地。
    它们是欧洲仅存的最大的陆地哺乳动物，他们喜欢做一些事情，例如从树上吃树皮，这种树皮可以选择性地杀死某些树木，从而使更多的光进入林地。我们经常用电锯做的事情，＆＃34;史密斯说。
  ＆＃34;它们显然还会产生大量的粪便。对于无脊椎动物，真菌和类似的东西，这真是太棒了，他说。
  野牛还可能有益于一种林地蝴蝶，名为``荒川贝母''（Heath Fritillary），该蝴蝶在春季末和初夏出现，并依靠一种称为普通小麦的植物作为食物。
  史密斯解释说，这种植物需要新近砍伐的林地才能生长，野牛可以通过放牧植被和剥去树皮来提供这种植物。 
因此，您已经拥有了欧洲最大的陆地哺乳动物，它对这只细小而又细小的蝴蝶产生了有益的影响，他说。
  史密斯说，荷兰的野牛已有15年之久了，史密斯说，当他访问那里的野牛项目时，他个人注意到栖息地健康状况有了很大改善。
  你不禁会注意到那里的鸟鸣声......到处都是蘑菇，到处都是蜥蜴和爬行动物，＆＃34;他说。
      护林员工作的关键部分将是保持动物的野性。
  ＆＃34;我们希望他们的行为完全自然，而不是[变成]人类使用的，＆＃34;史密斯说。
  野牛管理员将在远处监视动物，收集粪便样本以评估它们的健康，而无需接近它们。史密斯（Smith）说，管理人员将必须跟踪并跟踪人群，以跟上他们的工作以及去向。
  史密斯说，他们起初从小做起，以确保野牛一年四季都有足够的食物。 
通过这种方式，我们可以真正测试他们对环境的影响，并根据需要调整事物，并进行调整。 他说。  他还指出，野牛的繁殖速度很慢，每年每只雌性最多一头小牛，因此他们并不希望牛群的大小迅速膨胀-他说这是在当地获得支持的众多因素之一。  每个人实际上对此非常非常满意。 似乎每个人都很高兴看到野牛来了。
note_en:
As It Happens      It's a job in the great outdoors that doesn't require applicants to have done it before, but the successful candidates will need to be comfortable with horns, hooves and fur.
   A conservation project in England is seeking to hire two rangers to oversee a small herd of four to 10 European bison that will be introduced to the ancient Blean Woods near Canterbury in the spring of 2022. The area hasn't seen bison roaming the wild for an estimated 6,000 years.
  Stan Smith is the wilder landscapes manager at the Kent Wildlife Trust, a group that has partnered with the Wildwood Trust to create the Wilder Blean project to reintroduce the bison. He hopes the initiative will increase ecological diversity there and is looking for humans to help make it happen.
  "We're not expecting people to have bison experience in the U.K., that's for sure, because you couldn't get any yet. There aren't any here," he told  As It Happens host Carol Off from Hastings, England.
  "But what we're looking for in people is somebody who has a really good sort of empathy with animals, that they're probably used to managing large grazing herbivores, things like cattle or horses, and somebody who really understands ... the ecology of the area," he said.
    Smith says that previously, the area would have been home to the steppe bison, which is extinct.
  The new bison herd will have about 200 hectares of land to graze — about half of the woodland's 400 hectares. They'll be kept in by an electric fence, according to Smith.
  "It's a really nice big size of woodland, one of our largest areas of ancient woodland that we still have ... in the south of England," said Smith.
    The hope is that the animals will benefit the woodland's ecosystem through their natural grazing behaviours.
  Smith says bison are natural "ecosystem engineers" that modify their habitats just by being there.
    "They are Europe's largest remaining land mammal and ... they love to do things like eat bark off trees, which can sort of selectively kill off certain trees, allowing more light to the woodland floor — the sort of thing that we try to do with chainsaws quite frequently," said Smith.
  "They also obviously produce a lot of dung. And that's fantastic for invertebrates and for fungi and things like that," he said.
  The bison may also benefit a type of woodland butterfly, the Heath Fritillary, which emerges in late spring and early summer and relies on a plant called the common cow-wheat for food.
  Smith explained that the plant requires freshly-cleared woodland areas to grow, which the bison could provide through grazing on vegetation and stripping bark.
  "So you've got Europe's largest land mammal having a beneficial impact on this on this tiny, tiny little butterfly," he said.
  The Netherlands have had wild bison for about 15 years now, Smith says, and he personally noticed a huge improvement in habitat health when he visited bison projects there.
  "You just can't help but notice the birdsong that's there ... the mushrooms that are growing everywhere and the lizards and reptiles that are all around, " he said.
      A key part of the job for the rangers will be to keep the animals wild.
  "We want them to be behaving entirely naturally and not [become] used to humans," said Smith.
  The bison managers will monitor the animals from a distance, collecting dung samples to assess their health without approaching them. Smith said the managers will have to track and follow the herd to keep up with what they're doing and where they're going.
  Smith says they are starting small at first to make sure the bison will have enough food year-round.
  "That way we can really test the impact that they're having on the environment and tweak things as we go along if we have to," he said.
  He also noted that bison breed slowly, at most one calf per female, per year, so they don't expect the herd size to balloon rapidly — one of many factors he says has helped foster support for the project locally.
  "Everyone's actually very, very happy with it. It seems everyone's really excited to see the bison come."
title: 野牛游骑兵想监督英国牧群-无需野牛经验
title_en: Bison rangers wanted to oversee U.K. herd – no bison experience necessary

I know a number of folks use  The Great Suspender to automatically suspendinactive browser tabs in Chrome. Apparently recent versions of this extensionhave been  taken over by a shady anonymous entity and is now flagged by Microsoft as malware. Notably the most recentversion of the extension (v7.1.8) has added integrated analytics that cantrack all of your browsing activity across all sites. Yikes.
   Disable analytics tracking by opening the extension options forThe Great Suspender and unchecking“Automatic deactivation of any kind of tracking”.
 Pray that the shady developer doesn’t issue a malicious update to The Great Suspender later.(There’s no sensible way to disable updates of an individual extension.)
  Download the  latest good version of The Great Suspender (7.1.6) from GitHub,and move it to some permanent location outside your Downloads folder.(It should be commit 9730c09.)
 Load your downloaded copy as  an unpacked extension.(This copy will not auto-update to future untrusted versions of the extension.)

我们与天才亲密接触了五年。这是我们学到的12件事。
   在过去的五年中，我们与地球上最聪明的人之一生活在一起。
  看，我们刚刚出版了克劳德·香农博士的传记。他是您从未听说过的最重要的天才，他的才智与爱因斯坦（Albert Einstein）和牛顿（Isaac Newton）相当。
 我们和他一起度过了五年。可以毫不夸张地说，在此期间，我们与已故的克劳德·香农在一起的时间比与许多在世的朋友在一起的时间还多。他变成了我们脑海中空余房间的室友，这个家伙总是四处徘徊并占据着我们的头部空间。
 是的，我们是讲述他的故事的人，但讲故事时，他也影响了我们。天才具有与世界互动的独特方式，如果您花足够的时间检查他们的习惯，就会发现他们光彩照人的行为。无论我们是否打算这样做，了解克劳德·香农的生活都为我们提供了如何更好地生活的经验教训。
 这就是本文的内容。这是我们室友留下的好东西。
  他的名字可能没有响。不用担心，我们刚开始时也不知道他是谁。 
在工程和数学界，香农是一个受人尊敬的人物。克劳德·香农（Claude Shannon）在1930年代和1940年代的工作为他赢得了“信息时代之父”的头衔。他在21岁时发表了有史以来最重要的硕士学位论文，解释了二进制开关如何执行逻辑。它为将来的所有数字计算机奠定了基础。
 他还没做完。他32岁那年出版了《传播的数学理论》，被称为“信息时代的大宪章”。香农（Shannon）的杰作发明了比特或信息的客观度量方法，并解释了数字代码如何使我们能够以完美的准确性压缩和发送任何消息。
   克劳德·香农（Claude Shannon）不仅是一个聪明的理论家，他还是一个非常肥沃，有趣，实用和富有创造力的人。有很多数学家和工程师撰写出色的论文。像香农一样，也很少有人像杂耍者，独轮车手，小玩意，一流的国际象棋手，密码破解者，专业选股者和业余诗人一样。
 在第二次世界大战期间，他致力于连接FDR和温斯顿·丘吉尔的最秘密的跨大西洋电话线路，并共同制造了世界上第一台可穿戴式计算机。他学会了驾驶飞机并弹奏爵士单簧管。他在自己的房子里架起了一堵假墙，只要按一下按钮就可以旋转，他还曾经建造了一个小工具，当它打开时，其唯一目的是打开，释放机械手并自行关闭。哦，他曾经在《时尚》杂志上刊登过一张照片。
   我们不是数学家也不是工程师；我们写书和演讲，而不是代码。这意味着我们在理解他的工作方面有着陡峭的学习曲线。
 但这很重要：我们必须从头开始学习所有内容，并使其在页面上有意义。如果我们以专家的身份来接触这本书，我们可能会被诱惑去深入研究香农定理，图表和证明的细节。
 但是因为我们是作为学习者来学习本书的，所以我们对一系列更广泛，更通俗的问题特别感兴趣：像克劳德·香农这样的人的工作方式如何？是什么塑造了这样的思想？这样的头脑有什么有趣的作用？无论发生什么，我们可以从中获得什么，使我们的追求变得更加辉煌？ 
克劳德·香农（Claude Shannon）对直接回答此类问题并不特别感兴趣。如果他还活着阅读这篇文章，他可能会嘲笑我们。他的想法是针对问题的热导导弹。早上起床的是剖析事物的运作方式，而不是专注于创造力和生产力。
 无论有多少人向他寻求建议，他都从未觉得自己从事过提供建议的业务。在担任教授期间，他对工作的指导方面特别紧张。他曾经抗议道：“我不能当顾问。” “我不能给任何人建议。我无权提供建议。”
 像往常一样，香农过于谦虚。即使他勉强和间接地完成了整个教学工作，他也可以教给我们很多东西。为此，我们将过去几年中从他那里学到的东西总结到了这篇文章中。无论如何，它并不是一个全面的清单，但我们希望它确实开始揭示这个未知的天才可以教给我们其他人关于思想，生活的知识。
   我们都知道社交媒体的持续干扰和嗡嗡的智能手机会如何破坏焦点和生产力。我们还知道，这个问题比20世纪中叶的美国要困难得多（是的，我们认为克劳德·香农对此有一些疏忽大意）。
 但是在任何时代，分心都是生活的永久特征，香农向我们展示了将注意力拒之门外不仅是获得随机焦点的问题。这是关于有意识地设计自己的生活和工作习惯，以最大程度地减少生活和工作习惯。
 一方面，香农不允许自己清理收件箱。他不想回信的信件进入了一个标有“我拖延了太久的信件”的垃圾箱。实际上，我们仔细研究了香农在华盛顿特区国会图书馆的来信，该信将他的论文归档在案—我们发现来信远远多于去信。节省下来的所有时间都花在了更多时间上以投入研究和修补工作。
  香农对他在办公室的时间保持了同样的态度，他的同事们经常期望他在办公室里发现自己的门是关着的（在贝尔实验室普遍开放的文化中很少见）。据我们所知，尚农的任何同事都没有将他记为粗鲁或不友善。但是他们的确记得他是一个重视他的隐私和安静思考时间的人。一位同事记得：“您会敲门，他会和您说话，否则他会自言自语。” 
另一方面，带着大胆的新主意或引人入胜的工程难题来到香农的同事们记得数小时的富有成效的对话。这就是说，香农与其他许多人一样，都在刻意思考如何利用自己的时间：激发想法，而不是闲聊。即使对于我们当中那些比Shannon更为外向的人（老实说，这几乎就是我们所有人），从他如何刻意和始终如一地将他的工作时间变成一个无干扰的区域中，我们仍然可以学到一些东西。
  在其数学工作中，香农具有跳到中心洞察力的品质，而后又保留了细节。正如他曾经解释过的那样，“我认为我比象征更直观。我试图去了解发生了什么。等式会在以后出现。”好像他在知道解决方案之前就已经解释了为什么是正确的。
 正如他的学生鲍勃·加拉格尔（Bob Gallager）回忆的那样，“他有一个奇怪的见解。他可以看穿一切。他会说，“这样的事情应该是正确的……”而他通常是对的……如果您没有精湛的直觉，就不可能用全盘手段来开发整个领域。”
 有时，这会给香农带来麻烦-学术数学家有时会指责他的工作不够严格。通常，尽管如此，他们的批评是错误的。数学家所罗门·戈洛姆布（Solomon Golomb）说：“实际上，香农对真正真实的事物具有几乎不屈不挠的直觉。”如果需要填写旅程的细节，那么目的地几乎总是正确的。
 当然，我们大多数人都不是天才，而且大多数人没有香农级的直觉。那么，有什么可以向他学习的吗？我们认为是这样的：即使我们的直觉不会引导我们发展诸如信息论之类的全新领域，但它们通常具有我们可以选择调和或闭嘴的智慧。
 担心缺少细节和中间步骤是确保我们直觉，错过创新突破的最佳机会的肯定方法。期望我们的大创意从前提到结论逻辑地展开，这是对创造性通常在实践中起作用的方式的误解。正如作家丽塔·梅·布朗（Rita Mae Brown）所说，“直觉是由于不耐烦而使逻辑悬而未决。”
 事后清理并填写详细信息是一回事。将我们向他人展示我们的想法的整洁方式，以及他人在文章，幻灯片或演讲中向我们展示他们的想法的另一种方式误认为是获取这些想法的混乱过程。等待整洁的突破通常意味着等待火车永不到达。 
诸如此类的许多文章都宣扬了指导的价值，而我们也不想太在意这一点。导师当然很重要。但是，很多有关导师制的文章都倾向于将导师视为您获得的东西：找到合适的聪明，成功的人来支持您的职业生涯，并且一切就绪。
 没那么简单。充分利用指导，不仅需要自信与可以帮助您改善发展的人接触。即使在感到不舒服，具有挑战性或违反直觉的情况下，也要谦虚地将指导放在心上。否则，有什么意义？
 香农最重要的导师可能是他在麻省理工学院的研究生院顾问瓦内瓦尔·布什，后者继续协调第二次世界大战期间美国的科学工作，并成为第一位总统科学顾问。布什认识到香农的天才，但他也做了导师应该做的事情–他以某种富有成效的方式将香农赶出了自己的舒适区。
  例如，在香农的硕士学位论文获得成功后，布什敦促香农撰写有关理论遗传学的博士学位论文，香农不得不从头开始学习，这与他花费了多年的工程和数学工作相去甚远。布什推动香农这样做，证明了他对自己的门徒挑战挑战能力的信任；香农同意证明他愿意伸张自己。
 香农可能会在那一刻做出一系列可能的回应（“遗传学，是吗？”）。但是布什知道他在做什么，香农谦虚到足以相信他的判断并接受指导。
 接受真正的指导在某种程度上是一种谦卑的举动：当您真正愿意相信导师看到您看不到的东西时，这才是最好的。毕竟，有一个原因是您首先找到了它们。谦虚地听。
  范内瓦尔·布什（Vannevar Bush）以另一种方式在香农留下了自己的烙印：他捍卫了广义而非专业的价值。正如他对一群麻省理工学院的教授所说： 
“如今，当人们趋向于如此紧密地专注于专业时，很高兴提醒我们，达·芬奇（Leonardo da Vinci）甚至本杰明·富兰克林（Benjamin Franklin）都无法同时拥有广泛而深入的可能性。我们这个专业的人，我们的老师，一定会被那些具有惊人才能的年轻人对科学的一个小角落感兴趣而对世界其他地方不感兴趣的趋势所感动。 。 。 。不幸的是，当一个聪明而富有创造力的头脑坚持生活在现代修道院的牢房中时。”
 布什鼓励香农避免使用各种牢房-香农随后的职业证明了他对这堂课的深刻理解。
 我们知道：布什的建议如今听起来可能不合时宜。我们职业生活中的许多压力促使我们不惜一切代价进行专业化，培养使我们在竞争中脱颖而出的一种利基技能，并不断努力。按照这种观点，兴趣广泛而不是深远的人基本上是认真的。更糟糕的是，它们注定要被知道如何真正专注的竞争对手所取代。
 这种观点会让香农大为恼火。我们认为，布什的通才福音与他产生了深深的共鸣，因为它符合香农的自然好奇心。他在自己所选择的领域之所以如此成功，不仅是因为他拥有无与伦比的智力，而且还因为他刻意保持自己的兴趣多样化。
 他杰出的硕士学位论文将他对布尔逻辑和计算机构建的兴趣结合在一起，这两个主题直到融化在香农的大脑中才被认为是完全无关的。他的信息论论文吸引了他对代码破解，语言和文学的迷恋。正如他曾经向布什解释的那样：
 “我一直在同时研究三个不同的想法，奇怪的是，这似乎比解决一个问题更有效率。”
 在深入研究这些知识追求的同时，香农通过采取一系列爱好来保持自己的头脑敏捷：爵士音乐，独轮车，杂耍，国际象棋，小玩意儿，业余诗歌等等。他是一个可以利用自己的才华在某个选定领域中钻研越来越多的人，整个职业生涯都在为同一主题绞尽脑汁。但是我们很幸运，他选择当个涉水手。 
涉猎的一部分是让自己自由在需要时停下来。香农天才，没有完成他开始的一切。虽然这可能与许多关于生产力的现代建议背道而驰，但我们认为其中确实存在智慧。香农会工作直到他感到满意为止，然后再继续做其他事情。在有些人看到异教徒的地方，我们看到了一个肥沃的头脑，他在继续前进之前确切地知道要走多远。
 甚至现代生产力运动的先驱蒂姆·费里斯（Tim Ferriss）都宣告知道何时折叠他们的重要性：“能够放弃无效的事情对于赢得胜利至关重要。”有这么多伟大的作家把未出版的小说藏在抽屉里是有原因的。如此之多的才华横溢的艺术家最终在其工作室中储存了一批未完成的绘画，这是有原因的。
   1961年，当他与香农（Shannon）合作制造一款开创性的可穿戴计算机，以击败轮盘赌上的房子时，埃德·索普（Ed Thorp）亲眼看到了香农的工作环境-特别是在香农做大量修补工作的大型家庭作坊中。
 这是索普描述研讨会的方式：“这是一个小工具的天堂……有数百种机械和电气类别，例如电动机，晶体管，开关，皮带轮，齿轮，电容器，变压器等等。”香农不厌其烦地弄脏自己的双手，不让机器零件和未完成的项目散落在各处，随着他的好奇心从一个项目跳到另一个项目。
  Shannon的更多学术追求也类似于该研讨会。他的阁楼上满是便条，未完成的文章以及在直纹纸上的“好问题”。
 一方面，我们会对他从未散布到世界各地的未完成的工作感到遗憾。另一方面，我们可以意识到，混乱是他所做的杰出工作的条件：香农没有投入精力来整理论文和工作空间，而是将其投入到研究象棋，机器人或投资策略中。称他为《无处不在的喜悦》的早期采用者。
  香农的兴趣广泛，这意味着他的见解有时需要一些时间才能实现。不幸的是，通常，他从来没有到处发表自己的发现。但是，如果他追随好奇心的趋势有时使他的生产力下降，那么他也有耐心在多年中不断恢复自己的最佳想法。 
他1948年撰写的信息理论论文将近十年。他在1939年刚刚完成研究生学习时，就首次想到了研究“用于智能传输的通用系统的某些基本属性，包括电话，广播，电视，电报等”的想法。从第一次提出该想法到其发表之间的几年，香农不仅可以更深入地研究信息，而且可以帮助美国第二次世界大战的工作，包括对高射炮和密码学的研究。但是，香农的信息理论继续发芽，即使他不得不在业余时间继续努力。
 后来，他回想起这段直觉。工作不是线性的；想法来了。 “有时候，这些事情……一个晚上，我记得我是在半夜醒来的，我有一个主意，我整夜熬夜从事这项工作。”香农的一位同事观察到，当他的信息论论文最终着陆时，“它是炸弹来的。”这是十年来严格训练的思想的总和，香农愿意让他的思想成熟的想法得到了回报。
  在我们这样的时代生活中，这可能是我们最难以接受的教训。我们沐浴在即时的满足中。等待十分钟，更不用说十年，对我们的工作有所反应的想法似乎几乎是荒谬的。但是对于创意，企业家和创意世界中的人们，可能没有其他有用的建议需要我们听听。天才需要时间。
 还请记住：克劳德·香农（Claude Shannon）十年来一直没有专职从事信息理论研究。在那些年中的很多年里，他一直都很忙。也许是最终的喧嚣。但是他坚持不懈的努力产生了他有史以来最重要的作品。
 如果我们坚持足够长的时间，在业余时间该怎么办？
  香农股份有限公司。他的贝尔实验室的同事中最多的人可以说，对于香农的社交生活，他“并不友善”-但他绝对不是党的一生。
 另一位同事布罗克韦·麦克米伦（Brockway McMillan）指出，“香农”对那种相当普遍的数学论证有某种不耐烦。他以不同于大多数人的方式解决问题。”香农不同的知识视野常常使他显得冷漠或不耐烦。正如麦克米兰（McMillan）所说：“他从不争论自己的想法。如果人们不相信他们，他会忽略那些人。” 
傲慢自大与自信自在之间有一条细线。香农通常停留在生产线的右边，因为他有足够的智力资源来支持自己的信心。但同样重要的是，他花了很多时间来培养这些资源，因为他永远不会陷入争夺地位，参与办公室政治或试图赢得每位批评家的困扰。对于他来说，解决问题的乐趣比这一切更有价值，因此，在选择相对较少的朋友时，他特意选择了那些乐于做同样的事情并帮助发扬光大的人。在他那边。
 在第二次世界大战期间，这些朋友包括艾伦·图灵（Alan Turing），在图灵进行实况考察以代表英国政府研究美国密码学的过程中，香农与他们进行了活跃的知识交流。在贝尔实验室，香农还与工程师邦尼·奥利弗（Barney Oliver）和约翰·皮尔斯（John Pierce）联系在一起，他们每个人都是信息技术史上的先驱人物。
 香农受益于相同的过程。他变得更加聪明和更具创造力，因为他几乎选择只与自己崇拜的聪明才智的人在一起。比我们大多数人更重要的是，他对朋友情有独钟，只选择了尽自己最大能力的朋友。
 香农的友善态度着眼于内容，而不仅仅是联系。当然，香农和他的朋友们度过了休闲时光，分享了他们的乐趣。但可能比许多朋友更多，他们还花时间谈论严肃而令人讨厌的事情。艾伦·图灵和克劳德·香农并没有谈天气。他们通过谈论人工智能来建立联系，而人工智能只有两个先驱者能做到。
  这对我们其余的非天才人士意味着什么？这并不意味着放弃

We got  up-close-and-personal with a genius for five years. Here are 12 things we learned.
   For the last five years, we lived with one of the most brilliant people on the planet.
  See, we just  published the biography of Dr. Claude Shannon. He’s the most important genius you’ve never heard of, a man whose intellect was on par with Albert Einstein and Isaac Newton.
 We spent five years with him. It’s not an exaggeration to say that, during that period, we spent more time with the  deceased Claude Shannon than we have with many of our  living friends. He became something like the roommate in the spare bedroom of our minds, the guy who was always hanging around and occupying our head space.
 Yes, we were the ones telling his story, but in telling it, he affected us, too. Geniuses have a unique way of engaging with the world, and if you spend enough time examining their habits, you discover the behaviors behind their brilliance. Whether or not we intended it to, understanding Claude Shannon’s life gave us lessons on how to better live our own.
 That’s what follows in this essay. It’s the good stuff our roommate left behind.
  His name may not ring a bell. Don’t worry, we didn’t know who he was either when we started.
  Within engineering and mathematics circles, Shannon is a revered figure. Claude Shannon’s work in the 1930s and 1940s earned him the title of “father of the information age.” At the age of 21, he published what’s been called the most important master’s thesis of all time, explaining how binary switches could do logic. It laid the foundation for all future digital computers.
 He wasn’t done. At the age of 32, he published “A Mathematical Theory of Communication,” which has been called “the Magna Carta of the information age.” Shannon’s masterwork invented the  bit, or the objective measurement of information, and explained how digital codes could allow us to compress and send any message with perfect accuracy.
   Claude Shannon wasn’t just a brilliant theoretical mind — he was a remarkably fertile, fun, practical, and inventive one as well. There are plenty of mathematicians and engineers who write great papers. There are fewer of them who, like Shannon, are also jugglers, unicyclists, gadgeteers, first-rate chess players, codebreakers, expert stock-pickers, and amateur poets.
 He worked on the top-secret transatlantic phone line connecting FDR and Winston Churchill during World War II and co-built what was arguably the world’s first wearable computer. He learned to fly airplanes and played the jazz clarinet. He rigged up a false wall in his house that could rotate with the press of a button, and he once built a gadget whose only purpose when it was turned on was to open up, release a mechanical hand, and turn itself off. Oh, and he once had a photo spread in  Vogue magazine.
   We aren’t mathematicians or engineers; we write  books and speeches, not code. That meant we had a steep learning curve in making sense of his work.
 But that was sort of the point: We had to learn everything from scratch and make it sensible on the page. Had we approached this book as experts, we might have been tempted to go deeper and deeper into the details of Shannon’s theorems, diagrams, and proofs.
 But because we approached this book as learners, we were particularly interested in a broader, more generalist set of questions: how does a mind like Claude Shannon’s work? What shapes a mind like that? What does a mind like that do for fun? What can we take from it to be just a bit more brilliant in our own pursuits, whatever they happen to be?
  Claude Shannon wasn’t especially interested in offering direct answers to questions like those. If he were alive to read this piece, he’d probably laugh at us. His mind was a heat-seeking missile targeting problems. What got him up in the morning was dissecting how things worked, not digressions into creativity and productivity.
 No matter how many people came to him for advice, he never felt that he was in the advice-giving business. During his days as a professor, he was particularly nervous about the mentoring aspect of the job. “I can’t be an advisor,” he once protested. “I can’t give advice to anybody. I don’t have the right to advise.”
 As usual, though, Shannon was being excessively modest. He can teach us  a lot, even if he approached the whole business of teaching reluctantly and indirectly. To that end, we’ve distilled what we’ve learned from him over these last few years into this piece. It isn’t a comprehensive list by any means, but it does begin, we hope, to reveal what this unknown genius can teach the rest of us about thinking — and living.
   We all know how the constant distractions of social media and buzzing smartphones destroy focus and productivity. We also know that the problem is considerably more difficult than it was in mid-20th-century America (and yes, we suppose Claude Shannon bears some inadvertent blame for this).
 But distractions are a permanent feature of life, in any era, and Shannon shows us that shutting them out isn’t just a matter of achieving random bursts of focus. It’s about consciously designing one’s life and work habits to minimize them.
 For one, Shannon didn’t allow himself to get caught up in clearing out his inbox. Letters he didn’t want to respond to went into a bin labeled “Letters I’ve Procrastinated On For Too Long.” In fact, we pored over Shannon’s correspondence at the Library of Congress in Washington, DC, which keeps his papers on file — and we found far more incoming letters than outgoing ones. All of that time saved was more time to plow back into research and tinkering.
  Shannon extended the same attitude to his time in the office, where his colleagues regularly expected to find his door closed (a rarity in Bell Labs’ generally open-door culture). None of Shannon’s colleagues, to our knowledge, remembered him as rude or unfriendly; but they do remember him as someone who valued his privacy and quiet time for thinking. One colleague remembered, “You would knock on the door and he would talk to you, but otherwise, he kept to himself.”
 On the other hand, colleagues who came to Shannon with bold new ideas or fascinating engineering puzzles remembered hours of productive conversations. That’s just to say that Shannon, as in so much else, was deliberate about how he invested his time: in stimulating ideas, not in small talk. Even for those of us who are more extraverted than Shannon was (and, to be honest, that’s nearly all of us), there’s something to learn from how deliberately and consistently he turned his work hours into a distraction-free zone.
  In his mathematical work, Shannon had a quality of leaping right to the central insight and leaving the details to be filled in later. As he once explained it, “I think I’m more visual than symbolic. I try to get a feeling of what’s going on. Equations come later.” It was as if he  saw solutions before he could explain why they were correct.
 As his student Bob Gallager recalled, “He had a weird insight. He could see through things. He would say, ‘Something like this should be true’…and he was usually right…You can’t develop an entire field out of whole cloth if you don’t have superb intuition.”
 Occasionally, this got Shannon in trouble — academic mathematicians sometimes accused him of being insufficiently rigorous in his work. Usually, though, their critiques were misguided. “In reality,” said the mathematician Solomon Golomb, “Shannon had almost unfailing instinct for what was actually  true.” If the details of the journey needed filling in, the destination was almost always correct.
 Most of us, of course, aren’t geniuses, and most of us don’t have Shannon-level intuition. So is there anything to learn from him here? We think there is: even if our intuitions don’t lead us to develop an entirely new field like information theory, they often have a wisdom that we can choose to tune into or to shut up.
 Worrying about missing details and intermediate steps is a sure way to shut our intuitions up, and to miss out on some of our best shots at creative breakthroughs. Expecting our big ideas to unfold logically from premise to conclusion is a misunderstanding of the way creativity usually works in practice. As the writer Rita Mae Brown put it, “Intuition is a suspension of logic due to impatience.”
 It’s one thing to clean up and fill in the details after the fact. It’s another thing to mistake the neat-and-tidy way we present our ideas to others, and others present their ideas to us — in an article, a slideshow, or a talk — for the messy process of getting to those ideas. Waiting for a neat-and-tidy breakthrough usually means waiting for a train that’s never arriving.
  A lot of articles like this preach the value of mentorship, and we don’t want to belabor the point.  Of course mentors matter. But a lot of writing about mentorship tends to treat a mentor as something you  acquire: find the right smart, successful person to back your career, and you’re all set.
 It’s not that simple. Making the most of mentorship doesn’t just require the confidence to approach someone whose guidance can make a difference in your development. It requires the humility to take that guidance to heart, even when it’s uncomfortable, challenging, or counterintuitive. Otherwise, what’s the point?
 Shannon’s most pivotal mentor was probably his graduate school advisor at MIT, Vannevar Bush, who went on to coordinate the American scientific effort in WWII and became the first presidential science advisor. Bush recognized Shannon’s genius, but he also did what mentors are supposed to do — he pushed Shannon out of his comfort zone in some productive ways.
  For instance, after the success of Shannon’s master’s thesis, Bush urged Shannon to write his PhD dissertation on theoretical genetics, a subject Shannon had to pick up from scratch and that was far afield from the engineering and mathematics he had spent years working on. That Bush pushed Shannon to do so testifies to his trust in his protege’s ability to rise to the challenge; that Shannon agrees testifies to his willingness to stretch himself.
 There’s a whole set of possible responses Shannon  might have had to that moment (“Genetics, huh?”). But Bush knew what he was doing, and Shannon was humble enough to trust his judgment and let himself be mentored.
 Accepting real mentorship is, in part, an act of humility: The best of it comes when you’re actually willing to trust that mentor sees something you don’t see. There’s a reason, after all, that you sought them out in the first place. Be humble enough to listen.
  Vannevar Bush left his imprint on Shannon in another way: he defended the value of generalizing over specializing. As he told a group of MIT professors:
 “In these days, when there is a tendency to specialize so closely, it is well for us to be reminded that the possibilities of being at once broad and deep did not pass with Leonardo da Vinci or even Benjamin Franklin. Men of our profession — we teachers — are bound to be impressed with the tendency of youths of strikingly capable minds to become interested in one small corner of science and uninterested in the rest of the world. . . . It is unfortunate when a brilliant and creative mind insists upon living in a modern monastic cell.”
 Bush encouraged Shannon to avoid cells of all kinds — and Shannon’s subsequent career proves how deeply he absorbed the lesson.
 We know: Bush’s advice would probably sound unfashionable these days. So many of the pressures in our professional lives push us to specialize at all costs, to cultivate that one niche skill that sets us apart from the competition, and to keep hammering away at it. In this view, people whose interests are broad rather than deep are basically unserious. And what’s worse, they’re doomed to be overtaken by rivals who know how to really focus.
 It’s a view that would have exasperated Shannon. Bush’s generalist gospel struck such a deep chord with him, we think, because it accorded with Shannon’s natural curiosity. He was so successful in his chosen fields not just because of his raw intellectual horsepower, but because of how deliberately he kept his interests diverse.
 His remarkable master’s thesis combined his interests in Boolean logic and computer-building, two subjects that were considered entirely unrelated until they fused in Shannon’s brain. His information theory paper drew on his fascination with codebreaking, language, and literature. As he once explained to Bush:
 “I’ve been working on three different ideas simultaneously, and strangely enough it seems a more productive method than sticking to one problem.”
 And while he was diving into these intellectual pursuits, Shannon kept his mind agile by taking up an array of hobbies: jazz music, unicycling, juggling, chess, gadgeteering, amateur poetry, and more. He was a person who could have used his talents to burrow ever deeper and deeper into a chosen field, wringing out variations on the same theme for his entire career. But we’re fortunate that he chose to be a dabbler instead.
  Part of dabbling is giving yourself the freedom to stop when you want to. Talented as he was, Shannon didn’t finish everything he started. While that might also run counter to a lot of modern advice on productivity, we think there’s real wisdom in it. Shannon would work until he felt satisfied — and then move on to other things. Where some people see a dilettante, we see a fertile mind that knew exactly how far to take a project before moving on.
 Even the oracle of the modern productivity movement,  Tim Ferriss,  preaches the importance of knowing when to fold ’em: “Being able to quit things that don’t work is integral to being a winner.” There’s a reason so many great writers have an unpublished novel tucked away in the drawer. There’s a reason so many talented artists end up with a stockpile of unfinished paintings in their studios.
   When he partnered with Shannon in 1961 to build a pioneering wearable computer to beat the house at roulette, Ed Thorp got to see Shannon’s work environment up close — in particular, the huge home workshop where Shannon did the bulk of his tinkering.
 Here’s how Thorp described the workshop: it was “a gadgeteer’s paradise…There were hundreds of mechanical and electrical categories, such as motors, transistors, switches, pulleys, gears, condensers, transformers, and on and on.” Shannon had no qualms about getting his hands dirty, about leaving machine parts and half-finished projects scattered all over the place, of jumping from project to project as he followed his curiosity.
  Shannon’s more academic pursuits resembled that workshop, too. His attic was stuffed with notes, half-finished articles, and “good questions” on ruled paper.
 On one hand, we can regret the amount of unfinished work he never got around to sending out into the world. On the other, we can recognize that that chaos was the condition of the remarkable work he did do: rather than pouring mental energy into tidying up his papers and his workspace, Shannon poured it into investigating chess, robotics, or investment strategies. Call him an early adopter of  The Joy of Leaving Your Shit All Over the Place.
  Shannon’s breadth of interests meant that his insights sometimes took time to come to fruition. Often, unfortunately, he never got around to publishing his findings at all. But if his tendency to follow his curiosity wherever it led sometimes rendered him less productive, he also had the patience to keep coming back to his best ideas, over the course of years.
 His 1948 information theory paper was nearly a decade in the making. He was just finishing grad school in 1939 when he first conceived the idea of studying “some of the fundamental properties of general systems for the transmission of intelligence, including telephony, radio, television, telegraphy, etc.” The years between the first inkling of the idea and its publication would take Shannon not only deeper into the study of information, but into work aiding America’s WWII effort, including research on anti-aircraft gunnery and cryptography. But Shannon’s information theory continued to germinate, even when he had to work on it in his free time.
 Reflecting on this time later, he remembered the flashes of intuition. The work wasn’t linear; ideas came when they came. “These things sometimes…one night I remember I woke up in the middle of the night and I had an idea and I stayed up all night working on that.” When his information theory paper finally landed, observed one of Shannon’s colleagues, “it came as a bomb.” It was the sum of a decade’s disciplined thought, and Shannon’s willingness to let his ideas mature paid off.
  This is probably the hardest lesson for us to swallow, living in the age that we do. We bathe in instant gratification. And the idea of waiting ten minutes, let alone ten years, for some reaction to our work can seem almost outlandish. But for people in the creative, entrepreneurial, and idea-making worlds, there may be no more useful advice we need to hear. Genius takes time.
 Remember also: Claude Shannon wasn’t working on information theory full-time for ten years. It was, for many of those years, his side hustle. Maybe the ultimate side hustle. But his endurance in sticking with it yielded the most important work he’d ever produce.
 What could we do in our spare time if we stuck with something for long enough?
  Shannon kept limited company. The most one of his Bell Labs colleagues could say for Shannon’s social life was that he was “not unfriendly” — but he was certainly never the life of the party.
 Another colleague, Brockway McMillan, observed that Shannon “had a certain type of impatience with the kind of mathematical argument that was fairly common. He addressed problems differently from the way most people did.” And Shannon’s different intellectual wavelength often gave him an air of aloofness or impatience; as McMillan put it, “He never argued his ideas. If people didn’t believe in them, he ignored those people.”
 There’s a fine line between arrogance and serene self-confidence. Shannon generally stayed on the right side of the line, because he had the intellectual resources to back up his confidence. But just as importantly, he spent his time cultivating those resources, because he was never one to get caught up in jockeying for status, playing office politics, or trying to win over every critic. The pleasure of problem-solving was worth more to him than all of that, and so when it came to choosing his relatively small number of friends, he deliberately chose those who took pleasure in the same thing, and who helped to bring out the best in him.
 During World War II, those friends included Alan Turing, with whom Shannon struck up a lively intellectual exchange during Turing’s fact-finding trip to study American cryptography on behalf of the British government. At Bell Labs, Shannon also bonded with fellow engineers Barney Oliver and John Pierce, each of whom was a pioneering figure in the history of information technology in his own right.
 Shannon benefitted from the same process. He grew smarter and more creative because he chose to surround himself, almost exclusively, with people whose smarts and creativity he admired. More than most of us, he was deliberate in his friendships, only choosing friends who drew out his best.
 Shannon’s approach to friendship focused on the content, not just the connection. Sure, Shannon and his friends had casual moments and their share of fun; but probably more than many friends, they also spent time talking about serious and heady things. Alan Turing and Claude Shannon didn’t bond by talking about the weather. They bonded by talking about artificial intelligence as only two of its pioneers could.

title: 与克劳德·香农（Claude Shannon）一起工作1万小时：天才的思想，工作方式和生活
title_en: 10k Hours with Claude Shannon: How a Genius Thinks, Works, and Lives

阀门和五家PC视频游戏发行商因限制在欧洲经济区的跨境游戏销售而被欧盟委员会处以780万欧元（约950万美元）的罚款。欧盟委员会说，这些公司对大约100款PC视频游戏进行了地理封锁，从而阻止了它们在某些欧盟国家以外的地区被激活和播放。这违反了欧盟禁止单一类型壁垒的数字单一市场规则。
 欧盟委员会说，地理封锁是为了防止游戏在捷克，波兰，匈牙利，罗马尼亚，斯洛伐克，爱沙尼亚，拉脱维亚和立陶宛之外启动。地理封锁功能可防止生活在平均收入较高的欧盟国家/地区的游戏玩家省钱，而可以在较便宜的欧盟国家/地区购买游戏，然后在Steam上激活它们。欧盟委员会表示，激活密钥在2010年至2015年之间处于地理封锁状态。
  欧盟委员会竞争政策负责人说：“今天对Valve和五家PC视频游戏发行商的'地理封锁'做法实行制裁，这提醒我们，根据欧盟竞争法，禁止公司通过合同限制跨境销售。” “这种做法剥夺了欧洲消费者从欧盟数字单一市场中获得的利益，也剥夺了在欧盟范围内货比三家的机会。”
 总共有五家出版商被罚款。 Focus Home被罚款近290万欧元（约合350万美元），ZeniMax超过160万欧元（约合200万美元），Koch Media将近100万欧元（约合120万美元），Capcom 396,000欧元（约合480,000美元）和Bandai Namco被罚款。 340,000（约$ 410,000）。因为这些公司中的每一个都与调查合作，所以他们的罚款减少了10％至15％。但是，Valve选择不合作，被罚款超过160万欧元（约190万美元）。
 欧盟委员会早在2017年就对地理封锁的做法展开了正式调查，并正式要求Valve在2019年停止该做法。Valve先前曾辩称，只有少数游戏使用了区域锁定激活密钥，并指出它不应对发布者请求的区域锁定负责。它说，它在2015年终止了这一做法，只有少数例外。
note_en:
Valve and five PC video game publishers have been fined a total of €7.8 million (around $9.5 million)  by the European Commission for restricting cross-border game sales in the European Economic Area. The Commission said that the companies geo-blocked around 100 PC video games, preventing them from being activated and played outside certain EU countries. This broke the EU’s Digital Single Market rules which prohibit those types of barriers.
 The European Commission says the geo-blocking was to prevent games being activated outside Czechia, Poland, Hungary, Romania, Slovakia, Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania. Geo-blocking prevents gamers living in EU countries with higher average incomes from being able to save money buying them in EU states where they’re cheaper and then activating them on Steam. Activation keys were geo-blocked between 2010 and 2015, the European Commission said.
  “Today’s sanctions against the ‘geo-blocking’ practices of Valve and five PC video game publishers serve as a reminder that under EU competition law, companies are prohibited from contractually restricting cross-border sales,” the European Commission’s head of competition policy said. “Such practices deprive European consumers of the benefits of the EU Digital Single Market and of the opportunity to shop around for the most suitable offer in the EU.”
 Five publishers were fined in total. Focus Home was fined almost €2.9 million (around $ 3.5 million), ZeniMax over €1.6 million (around $2 million), Koch Media almost €1 million (around $1.2 million), Capcom €396,000 (around $480,000), and Bandai Namco €340,000 (around $410,000). Because each of these companies cooperated with the investigation, their fines were reduced by between 10 and 15 percent. Valve, however, chose not to cooperate, and was fined over €1.6 million (around $1.9 million).
 The European Commission opened its formal investigation into the practice of geo-blocking back in 2017, and formally asked Valve to stop the practice  back in 2019. Valve has previously argued that only a small number of games used region-locked activation keys, and argued it shouldn’t be liable for region locks requested by publishers. It said it ended the practice in 2015, with limited exceptions.

参与开源需要您放弃对商业开发的垄断。这是关于免费和开源软件的一个重要观点，它似乎使许多公司都对FOSS的理解感到挣扎，这是值得自己解决的问题。多年来，很明显，FOSS正在吃软件世界，公司正在努力弄清它们之间的关系。您在这一职位上必须面对的一个事实是，您不能垄断免费和开源软件的商业潜力。
 术语“开放源代码”被广泛接受，由“开放源代码定义”定义，其首要要求如下：
 [开源软件的分发条款]不应限制任何一方出售或赠送该软件，该软件是包含多个不同来源程序的聚合软件分发的一部分。许可不要求此类销售的特许权使用费或其他费用。
 这涵盖了“ FOSS”中的“ OSS”。 “ F”是指“自由软件”，并由此自由软件基金会资源覆盖：
 [如果程序的用户可以自由地出于任何目的运行该程序，则该程序是免费软件，无论出于何种目的，……并……都可以重新分发副本。
  “免费软件”并不意味着“非商业”。一个免费程序必须可用于商业用途，商业开发和商业发行。 […]不管您如何获得副本，您始终可以自由复制和更改软件，也可以自由出售副本。
 这是免费和开源软件的基本，不可协商的要求，如果想获得FOSS生态系统的好处，就必须面对一个现实。任何人都可以通过您的代码获利。这包括您和我，所有您的贡献者，您的竞争对手，亚马逊和Google，以及其他所有人。这是对知识产权通常如何运作的拒绝-版权法的存在是为了为您的企业和FOSS创建人为的垄断出于明文目的存在许可证。如果您是FOSS的新手，那么它将对您对IP所有权的理解完全陌生。 
除了您之外，其他人也可以从免费和开源软件作品中获利。有些人会将它们整合到自己的产品中进行销售，有些人将利用其开发专业知识并以顾问的身份出售他们的技能，有些人将以易于使用的方式将其重新包装，并向服务人员收取费用。其他人可能想出了更多创造性的方法来通过软件获利，例如撰写有关该书的书。它将为所有人创造财富，而不仅仅是原始作者。
 就业力正义而言，这是有道理的。使软件成为FOSS的最重要的优势之一是，全球社区可以对其进行改进。通过直接为您的代码做出贡献，以及通过软件蓬勃发展的社区，该软件变得超出了您组织的能力。如果其价值的总和不再对您的组织完全负责，那么也不应该由您的组织完全捕获对该价值的商业利用吗？这是您选择FOSS时要做的事情。
 您可以通过多种方法来影响他人使用您的FOSS软件的方式，主要是要确保其他所有人都遵守这一诺言。您无法阻止某人从您的软件中获利，但是您可以让他们与其他人分享他们的改进，您可以将其合并回原始产品中，以使其对所有人更具吸引力。 GPL系列许可证就是为此目的而设计的。 1个
 此外，如果您的企业是自由和开源软件的消费者，而不是生产者，则需要意识到您可能要遵守这些义务。这不是免费的午餐：您可能需要将自己的改进退还给社区。 FOSS许可证很重要，您应该让自己的企业以自由，开源软件的用户，贡献者和作者的身份去理解它们。
 FOSS正在吞噬整个世界，这是有充分理由对企业而言非常有吸引力的选择。这就是原因。它为每个人增加了财富。资本主义关注制造垄断，而FOSS关注的是软件财富的社会化创造。
 如果您想简要介绍GPL许可证，我已为SourceHut用户编写了一份简短指南。 ↩︎
note_en:
Participation in open source requires you to surrender your monopoly overcommercial exploitation. This is a profound point about free and open sourcesoftware which seems to be causing a lot of companies to struggle with theirunderstanding of the philosophy of FOSS, and it’s worth addressing on its own.It has been apparent for some years now that FOSS is eating the software world,and corporations are trying to figure out their relationship with it. One factthat you will have to confront in this position is that you cannot monopolizethe commercial potential of free and open source software.
 The term “open source” is broadly accepted as being defined by the  Open SourceDefinition, and its very first requirement is the following:
 [The distribution terms of open-source software] shall not restrict any partyfrom selling or giving away the software as a component of an aggregatesoftware distribution containing programs from several different sources. Thelicense shall not require a royalty or other fee for such sale.
 That covers the “OSS” in “FOSS”. The “F” refers to “free software”, and iscovered by  this Free Software Foundation resource:
 [A program is free software if the program’s users have] the freedom to runthe program as they wish, for any purpose, [… and to …] redistributecopies.
  “Free software” does not mean “noncommercial”. A free program must beavailable for commercial use, commercial development, and commercialdistribution. […] Regardless of how you got your copies, you always have thefreedom to copy and change the software, [and] to sell copies.
 This is an essential, non-negotiable requirement of free and open-sourcesoftware, and a reality you must face if you want to reap the benefits of theFOSS ecosystem.  Anyone can monetize your code. That includes you, and me, allof your contributors, your competitors, Amazon and Google, and everyone else.This is a rejection of how intellectual property typically works —copyright laws exist for the express purpose of creating an artificial monopolyfor your business, and FOSS licenses exist for the express purpose of breakingit. If you’re new to FOSS, it is going to be totally alien to your understandingof IP ownership.
 It’s quite common for people other than you to make money from your free andopen source software works. Some will incorporate them into their own productsto sell, some will develop an expertise with it and sell their skills as aconsultant, some will re-package it in an easy-to-use fashion and charge peoplefor the service. Others might come up with even more creative ways to monetizethe software, like writing books about it. It will create wealth for everyone,not just the original authors.
 This makes sense in terms of karmic justice, as it were. One of the mostimportant advantages of making your software FOSS is that the global communitycan contribute improvements back to it. The software becomes more than yourorganization can make it alone, both through direct contributions to your code,and through the community which blossoms around it. If the sum of its value isno longer entirely accountable to your organization, is it not fair that thecommercial exploitation of that value shouldn’t be entirely captured by yourorganization, either? This is the deal that you make when you choose FOSS.
 There are ways that you can influence how others use your FOSS software, mainlyhaving to do with making sure that everyone else keeps this same promise. Youcannot stop someone from making money from your software, but you  can obligatethem to share their improvements with everyone else, which you can incorporateback into the original product to make it more compelling for everyone. The  GPLfamily of licenses is designed for this purpose.  1
 Furthermore, if your business is a consumer of free and open source software,rather than a producer, you need to be aware that you may be subject to thoseobligations. It’s not a free lunch: you may be required to return yourimprovements to the community. FOSS licenses are important, and you should makeit your business to understand them, both as a user, contributor, and author offree and open source software.
 FOSS is eating the world, and it’s a very attractive choice for businesses for agood reason. This is the reason. It increases wealth for everyone. Capitalismconcerns itself with making monopolies — FOSS instead concerns itself withthe socialized creation of software wealth.
 If you want a brief introduction to GPL licenses, I have written  a short guide for SourceHut users.  ↩︎
